title: To the North
author: Max Berengaut
body: To the North is a novel written by the talented early 20th century British author Elizabeth Bowen. Its plot centers on two romantic affairs and the friendship between the two lead women. I was very much enjoying this book until I realized the writer really believed the upper class deserved their status over the "poor people." Still, some of the writing, particularly in depicting scenes and conversations between all of the characters in very well-sketched rooms, was very entertaining. Her style is a little much, I can't imagine she doesn't grow out of the inaccessibility somewhat further along in her career, as this is an early novel. Despite the clutter, it's nice to read someone grasping for loose psychological notions out of sport. Also, I think she really understand humans very well. I was made somewhat to understand her writing focuses on romantic desolation, and this does but not really in a way I very much enjoyed, as Emmeline does not live with the grief at all really which is the true furnace for that feeling — staying with it. Another thought I had was that the characters seemed very modern and contemporary by my standards except for the social rules on sex and marriage. "To be with her, so nearly to love her was to lend oneself wholly to an illusion, to hang in a drop of light in the lustres along her mantelpiece, to be reflected for less than a moment, like a bird's shadow flashing across a mirror, in her dazzling ignorance of oneself."
